title: Parabolic-growth universality and its nucleation-driven breakdown across lithium-battery anode chemistries
abstract: Solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) growth is widely modeled cell-by-cell with chemistry-specific closures, yet its underlying kinetic scaling is rarely tested across chemistries. By compiling cycle-resolved data from public long-cycle datasets covering four anode configurations -- graphite, silicon composite, lithium metal, and anode-free -- we show that the cumulative interphase-loss index Lambda_int obeys the parabolic law Lambda_int = A_chem * sqrt(1 - Theta_Li) in three of the four chemistries, with an exponent indistinguishable from alpha = 1/2 within experimental uncertainty. The chemistry-specific prefactor A_chem spans an order of magnitude, but the diffusion-limited parabolic kinetics is preserved. The fourth chemistry, anode-free configurations, deviates with a super-parabolic exponent alpha approx 0.77, consistent with a nucleation-controlled growth regime. We rationalize the result using the Tammann-Deal-Grove parabolic-growth framework adapted to interphase formation and identify the conditions under which universality is recovered. The observed regularity reduces SEI modeling complexity to a single rate constant per chemistry and provides a sharp falsifiable test for next-generation cell formats.
summary: This paper demonstrates that SEI growth in most lithium-battery anodes follows a universal parabolic law, simplifying the modeling of battery degradation. However, it identifies that anode-free configurations deviate from this pattern due to nucleation-driven kinetics, providing a new way to test next-generation battery designs.
main_problem: The study investigates whether the kinetic scaling of solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) growth follows a universal parabolic law across different lithium-battery anode chemistries.
main_result: The researchers find that three out of four anode configurations (graphite, silicon, and lithium metal) follow a parabolic growth law, while anode-free configurations deviate due to a nucleation-controlled growth regime.
method: The study performs a meta-analysis of cycle-resolved data from public long-cycle datasets and applies the Tammann-Deal-Grove parabolic-growth framework to analyze kinetic scaling.
model_or_system: The system consists of various lithium-ion battery anode configurations, specifically graphite, silicon composite, lithium metal, and anode-free cells, focusing on the growth kinetics of the SEI layer.
key_observables: The cumulative interphase-loss index (Lambda_int) and the growth exponent (alpha).
important_parameters: The chemistry-specific prefactor (A_chem) and the lithium utilization/capacity parameter (Theta_Li).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the growth kinetics can be modeled using an adaptation of the classical Tammann-Deal-Grove framework for interphase formation.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of SEI scaling, presents a comparative analysis of four anode chemistries using compiled datasets, identifies the breakdown of parabolic growth in anode-free cells, and provides a theoretical rationalization using the Tammann-Deal-Grove framework.
analysis_basis: abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
